verdict: All three posts and the newsletter meet the depth standard. Post 1 (choice architecture) is the strongest pillar of the series, with a concrete before-and-after appointment sequence that gives operators something they can use immediately. Post 2 (default effect) makes a genuinely counterintuitive point about leading-low proposals in a way that will resonate with operators who've experienced the renewal conversion problem. Post 3 (framing) is direct and practical. The newsletter builds meaningfully on the posts with the status quo bias section being the strongest additional material.
strengths: Post 1 converts an abstract concept into a concrete appointment sequence with before and after structure
Post 2 uses a specific example (6-month vs 12-month default) that operators will recognize immediately
Post 3 hook ('Our fee is 20% and you keep 80% are the same sentence') is strong and will stop the scroll
Newsletter's status quo bias section introduces diagnostic questions operators can use in competitive situations
Salience section in newsletter gives a specific, vivid owner story example that is more useful than an abstract recommendation
No em dashes throughout, CTA intact on all posts
problems: Post 2 body is slightly long and could trim the 'THE PROBLEM WITH LEADING LOW' section by one paragraph
The autonomy preservation section in the newsletter is the weakest of the newsletter additions -- slightly philosophical for this audience
